question_text: Chickpea (Cicer arietinum), popularly called gram or chana, is India's most important pulse crop contributing nearly 45% of total pulse production — which Indian state currently holds the top position in chickpea production?
options: A) Rajasthan
B) Madhya Pradesh
C) Maharashtra
D) Uttar Pradesh
explanation: Madhya Pradesh is India's largest chickpea-producing state, contributing nearly 40% of national chickpea output. Its black cotton soil, suitable rabi climate, and extensive area under chickpea drive this leadership. Rajasthan, Maharashtra, and Karnataka are also significant producers. India is the world's largest chickpea producer (over 70% of global production), but also imports pulses due to rising domestic demand, which motivated the Pulses Revolution and NFSM-Pulses mission.
elimination_hints: MP's black cotton soil (regur) areas like Malwa plateau are ideal for chickpea.
UP is a major wheat/rice state but ranks lower in pulses.
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Pigeonpea (Cajanus cajan), popularly called tur or arhar, plays a critical role in Indian pulse basket especially as dal — which of the following pigeonpea varieties is a high-yielding determinate type developed for short-duration cultivation?
options: A) Pusa Bold (brassica oilseed variety)
B) ICPL 87 (early, determinate short-duration pigeonpea from ICRISAT)
C) JS 335 (soybean variety)
D) Bhavani (rice variety)
explanation: ICPL 87 (commonly known as 'Pragati') is an early-maturing (120–130 days), determinate pigeonpea variety developed by ICRISAT, suitable for short-duration cultivation and wheat intercropping systems. Pusa Bold is a mustard variety, JS 335 is a widely grown soybean cultivar from JNKVV Jabalpur, and Bhavani is a rice variety. ICRISAT's pigeonpea improvement programme has released many successful early varieties for Indian farmers.
elimination_hints: Pusa Bold — the word 'bold' commonly refers to mustard/brassica seed size.
JS 335 — the 'JS' prefix is typical of Jabalpur soybean series.
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Soybean (Glycine max), popularly called the 'golden bean' due to its high protein and oil content, is the most important kharif oilseed in India — which state accounts for nearly 50% of India's soybean production?
options: A) Maharashtra
B) Madhya Pradesh
C) Rajasthan
D) Karnataka
explanation: Madhya Pradesh is known as the 'Soybean Bowl of India', contributing nearly 45–50% of national soybean production, followed by Maharashtra and Rajasthan. Soybean was introduced to India in the 1960s and found an ideal niche in MP's Malwa plateau with well-drained soils and assured monsoon. JS 335 from JNKVV Jabalpur is the most widely grown variety. Soybean is India's leading vegetable oil source after mustard and groundnut.
elimination_hints: The nickname 'Soybean Bowl' directly identifies MP.
Rajasthan is known for mustard/bajra but not soybean leadership.
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Groundnut (Arachis hypogaea) exhibits a unique botanical phenomenon called geocarpy that distinguishes it from other legumes — what is geocarpy in the context of groundnut pod development?
options: A) Aerial fruit formation on the above-ground flowering stems like peas and beans
B) Subterranean pod development after the fertilised gynophore (peg) penetrates the soil
C) Cross-pollination through wind and insect agents like most dicot crops
D) Seed dormancy regulated by day length and soil temperature fluctuations
explanation: Geocarpy is the unique botanical feature of groundnut wherein after self-pollination of aerial flowers, the fertilised ovary elongates into a 'peg' (gynophore) that grows downward and penetrates the soil. Pod (fruit) development happens entirely underground. This is why groundnut is also called 'earthnut' or 'monkey nut'. It requires well-drained, light sandy soils for easy peg penetration and pod development. Gypsum is applied at the pegging stage to ensure proper pod filling.
elimination_hints: The prefix 'geo-' means earth/soil — directly points to underground phenomenon.
A describes normal legumes — the whole point is that groundnut is unique.
subject: Agriculture

question_text: The Yellow Revolution in India refers to the dramatic increase in oilseed production during the 1986–1990 period, reducing import dependency for edible oils — which programme was primarily responsible for driving this revolution?
options: A) Operation Flood dedicated to dairy development nationwide
B) Technology Mission on Oilseeds (TMO) launched in 1986
C) National Horticulture Mission promoting fruit and vegetable expansion
D) National Agricultural Cooperative Marketing Federation reform package
explanation: The Yellow Revolution was spearheaded by the Technology Mission on Oilseeds (TMO), launched in 1986 under the leadership of Sam Pitroda. The mission integrated research, extension, input supply, and marketing to boost nine oilseeds (groundnut, mustard, soybean, sunflower, sesame, safflower, niger, linseed, castor). It reduced India's edible oil imports significantly. Operation Flood drove the White Revolution (milk); the other options are unrelated to oilseed development.
elimination_hints: Operation Flood is exclusively associated with milk/White Revolution.
Horticulture Mission targets fruits and vegetables — not oilseeds.
subject: Agriculture

question_text: The recommended NPK fertiliser dose for high-yielding dwarf paddy rice varieties under irrigated conditions in the Indo-Gangetic plains is approximately which combination of N, P₂O₅, and K₂O per hectare?
options: A) 30–40 kg N, 20 kg P₂O₅, 20 kg K₂O per hectare
B) 120 kg N, 60 kg P₂O₅, 40 kg K₂O per hectare
C) 250 kg N, 150 kg P₂O₅, 120 kg K₂O per hectare
D) 60 kg N only without any phosphorus or potassium application
explanation: The standard recommended dose for high-yielding irrigated rice in the Indo-Gangetic plains is approximately 120:60:40 kg NPK/ha. Nitrogen is applied in three splits (basal 50%, tillering 25%, panicle initiation 25%). Option A is too low for HYV; option C is excessive and economically irrational; option D misses P and K which are essential for root development and disease resistance. Slightly higher doses (150:75:60) are used for hybrid rice.
elimination_hints: 30 kg N is typical of rainfed traditional varieties — far below HYV requirements.
250 kg N would exceed recovery efficiency and cause lodging/pollution.
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Karnal bunt (Tilletia indica) is a regulatory quarantine disease of wheat that causes significant concerns in India's wheat export market because of its specific transmission characteristic — how is this disease primarily spread?
options: A) Through infected soil only, without any seed-borne phase
B) Through teliospores carried on seed surface and in soil, affecting seed quality but rarely yield
C) Through insect vectors like aphids and leafhoppers
D) Through systemic mycelium in the vascular tissue causing vascular wilt
explanation: Karnal bunt is caused by the smut fungus Tilletia indica, whose teliospores survive on seed surface and in soil. The fungus partially infects grains during flowering, converting only part of the kernel to a black spore mass with a characteristic fishy smell. Yield loss is minor but infected grain becomes unfit for human consumption. Many countries impose quarantine restrictions, causing export losses. It is not an insect-transmitted or vascular wilt disease.
elimination_hints: C and D describe completely different disease types (viral/vascular) — not smuts.
The quarantine/export issue is about seed contamination — points to seed-borne nature.
subject: Agriculture

question_text: The Food Corporation of India (FCI) was established in 1965 as the primary government agency responsible for food grain operations — which of the following is NOT a core function of FCI in India's food security architecture?
options: A) Procurement of paddy and wheat at MSP from farmers through mandis
B) Scientific storage of food grains in buffer stocks across depots
C) Distribution of grains to state governments for the Public Distribution System
D) Direct cash transfer of MSP amount to farmers' Aadhaar-linked bank accounts
explanation: FCI's core functions are: (1) procurement of paddy, wheat (and sometimes coarse grains) at MSP; (2) scientific storage in buffer stocks (operational + strategic reserves); (3) movement of grains across deficit regions; and (4) distribution to states for PDS. Direct cash transfers (DBT) are handled by state governments and banking channels via schemes like PM-KISAN, not by FCI. FCI deals with physical grain management, not direct cash disbursements.
elimination_hints: A, B, and C are FCI's textbook mandate — all three are correct functions.
Direct cash transfer is an NPCI/banking system function — unrelated to FCI's physical operations.
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Rapeseed and mustard (collectively called 'toria' and 'rai') are important rabi oilseeds in India contributing nearly 30% of domestic edible oil production — which state is the largest producer of mustard?
options: A) Rajasthan
B) Haryana
C) Uttar Pradesh
D) West Bengal
explanation: Rajasthan is India's largest mustard-producing state, accounting for nearly 40% of national mustard/rapeseed production. Its semi-arid winter climate, low rainfall, and suitable soils favour mustard cultivation. UP, Haryana, MP, and West Bengal are also significant producers. Pusa Bold, RH 30, and Varuna are popular varieties. Mustard is second only to groundnut and soybean in India's oilseed output.
elimination_hints: Rajasthan's semi-arid conditions favour mustard as the dominant rabi oilseed.
West Bengal grows some mustard but ranks much lower than Rajasthan.
subject: Agriculture

question_text: The National Food Security Mission (NFSM) was launched in 2007 with the primary objective of increasing production of specific crops through area expansion and productivity enhancement — which crops were included in the original NFSM programme?
options: A) Fruits, vegetables, and spices under the horticulture basket
B) Rice, wheat, pulses, and coarse cereals (with later additions)
C) Cotton, jute, and sugarcane as commercial crops
D) Tea, coffee, and rubber as plantation crops
explanation: NFSM was launched in 2007–08 to increase production of rice, wheat, and pulses by 10, 8, and 2 million tonnes respectively by 2011–12, through focused interventions in identified districts. The mission was extended in 12th and subsequent Plans to include coarse cereals (nutri-cereals/millets) and commercial crops like cotton, jute, and sugarcane in specific components. The core original focus was on food grain self-sufficiency — rice, wheat, and pulses.
elimination_hints: The word 'Food Security' points to staple food grains — not plantation or horticulture crops.
A specifically mentions horticulture — covered under a different mission (NHM).
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Match each major Indian pulse or oilseed crop with its correct leading producing state based on current agricultural statistics published by the Ministry of Agriculture.
column_b: Gujarat — accounts for nearly 40% of national production with Saurashtra as the core area
Maharashtra — largest producer especially in Vidarbha and Marathwada regions
Madhya Pradesh — 'Soybean Bowl' contributing nearly 50% of national output
Madhya Pradesh — contributes around 40% of national output driven by the Malwa plateau

correct_mapping.Chickpea (chana/gram): Madhya Pradesh — contributes around 40% of national output driven by the Malwa plateau
correct_mapping.Pigeonpea (arhar/tur): Maharashtra — largest producer especially in Vidarbha and Marathwada regions
correct_mapping.Soybean: Madhya Pradesh — 'Soybean Bowl' contributing nearly 50% of national output
correct_mapping.Groundnut: Gujarat — accounts for nearly 40% of national production with Saurashtra as the core area

options: A) Chickpea→MP/Malwa; Pigeonpea→Maharashtra/Vidarbha; Soybean→MP/Soybean Bowl; Groundnut→Gujarat/Saurashtra
B) Chickpea→Gujarat/Saurashtra; Pigeonpea→MP/Malwa; Soybean→Maharashtra/Vidarbha; Groundnut→MP/Soybean Bowl
C) Chickpea→Maharashtra/Vidarbha; Pigeonpea→Gujarat/Saurashtra; Soybean→MP/Malwa; Groundnut→MP/Soybean Bowl
D) Chickpea→MP/Soybean Bowl; Pigeonpea→MP/Malwa; Soybean→Gujarat/Saurashtra; Groundnut→Maharashtra/Vidarbha
explanation: Madhya Pradesh tops both chickpea (Malwa plateau) and soybean (Soybean Bowl); MP appears twice because it dominates rabi pulses and kharif oilseeds alike. Maharashtra leads in pigeonpea (Vidarbha and Marathwada black soil regions). Gujarat is India's top groundnut producer with Saurashtra's sandy soil and dry climate being ideal. Note the distinction — MP has two different regional specialisations for different seasons.
elimination_hints: Gujarat + Saurashtra is a classic geographical pairing for groundnut.
'Soybean Bowl' is MP's well-known nickname — a direct clue.
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Match each major plant disease with its causal organism and the crop it affects most severely under field conditions in Indian agriculture.
column_b: Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae — water-soaked yellow lesions along leaf margins spreading downward
Tilletia indica — partial kernel infection with fishy smell affecting seed quality in wheat
Xanthomonas citri subsp. citri — raised corky lesions on leaves, fruits, and twigs of citrus
Tobamovirus — mosaic mottling and leaf crinkling affecting tobacco, tomato, and chilli

correct_mapping.Karnal bunt: Tilletia indica — partial kernel infection with fishy smell affecting seed quality in wheat
correct_mapping.Bacterial blight of rice: Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae — water-soaked yellow lesions along leaf margins spreading downward
correct_mapping.Tobacco mosaic virus (TMV): Tobamovirus — mosaic mottling and leaf crinkling affecting tobacco, tomato, and chilli
correct_mapping.Citrus canker: Xanthomonas citri subsp. citri — raised corky lesions on leaves, fruits, and twigs of citrus

options: A) Karnal bunt→Tilletia/wheat; BB rice→Xanthomonas oryzae; TMV→Tobamovirus; Citrus canker→Xanthomonas citri
B) Karnal bunt→Xanthomonas citri; BB rice→Tilletia/wheat; TMV→Xanthomonas oryzae; Citrus canker→Tobamovirus
C) Karnal bunt→Tobamovirus; BB rice→Xanthomonas citri; TMV→Tilletia/wheat; Citrus canker→Xanthomonas oryzae
D) Karnal bunt→Xanthomonas oryzae; BB rice→Tobamovirus; TMV→Xanthomonas citri; Citrus canker→Tilletia/wheat
explanation: Karnal bunt is a seed-borne smut caused by Tilletia indica in wheat. Bacterial blight of rice is caused by Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae, showing water-soaked lesions that turn yellow to straw-coloured along leaf margins. TMV is a virus (Tobamovirus) known for its stability and the first virus ever discovered (by Ivanovsky, 1892), affecting solanaceous crops. Citrus canker is caused by Xanthomonas citri subsp. citri, producing raised corky lesions — a major regulatory disease of citrus.
elimination_hints: The name 'TMV' stands for Tobacco Mosaic Virus — self-evident for virus category.
Both Karnal bunt and citrus canker are regulatory diseases — but different pathogens (fungal vs bacterial).
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Match each major crop with the standard NPK fertiliser recommendation (N:P₂O₅:K₂O in kg per hectare) for high-yielding varieties under irrigated conditions as recommended by ICAR.
column_b: 120:60:40 kg/ha with N applied in three split doses
150:60:40 kg/ha with basal P and K plus three split N doses
120:60:40 kg/ha with P and K basal and N in two or three splits
250:115:115 kg/ha — very high NPK requirement with multiple N splits over long duration

correct_mapping.Rice (irrigated HYV): 120:60:40 kg/ha with N applied in three split doses
correct_mapping.Wheat (timely-sown irrigated): 120:60:40 kg/ha with P and K basal and N in two or three splits
correct_mapping.Maize (irrigated hybrid): 150:60:40 kg/ha with basal P and K plus three split N doses
correct_mapping.Sugarcane (plant crop): 250:115:115 kg/ha — very high NPK requirement with multiple N splits over long duration

options: A) Rice→120:60:40 (three splits); Wheat→120:60:40; Maize→150:60:40; Sugarcane→250:115:115
B) Rice→250:115:115; Wheat→150:60:40; Maize→120:60:40; Sugarcane→120:60:40 (three splits)
C) Rice→150:60:40; Wheat→250:115:115; Maize→120:60:40 (three splits); Sugarcane→120:60:40
D) Rice→120:60:40; Wheat→250:115:115; Maize→120:60:40 (three splits); Sugarcane→150:60:40
explanation: Standard NPK recommendations: rice HYV and wheat are around 120:60:40 kg/ha; maize hybrids get slightly higher N (150 kg) for cob development. Sugarcane, being a long-duration (10–12 month) and high-biomass crop, requires very high doses of around 250:115:115 kg/ha split across the growing season. Nitrogen is universally split to improve recovery efficiency and reduce losses. Actual doses vary by soil test and region.
elimination_hints: Sugarcane's long duration and massive biomass logically require the highest NPK.
Rice and wheat both have similar 120:60:40 doses — a common textbook fact.
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Analyse the Assertion and Reason below about the rice-wheat cropping system and determine the correct relationship between the two statements about Indian agriculture.
assertion: The rice-wheat cropping system practised over 10 million hectares in the Indo-Gangetic plains has shown signs of yield stagnation and soil degradation in recent decades.
reason: Continuous mono-cropping of the same two cereals depletes soil organic matter and specific micronutrients like zinc, creates adverse soil structure from puddling, and builds up pest and weed loads specific to the rotation.
options: A) Both Assertion and Reason are correct, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
B) Both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
C) Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect
D) Assertion is incorrect
explanation: The assertion is correct — yield plateauing in rice-wheat systems is well-documented, particularly in Punjab, Haryana, and western UP. The reason correctly explains the causes: repeated cereal-cereal cycling depletes soil organic matter, causes Zn and other micronutrient deficiencies (especially in alkaline soils after puddling), destroys soil structure through annual puddling, and leads to pest/weed build-up (Phalaris minor resistance in wheat, yellow stem borer in rice). These factors collectively stagnate yields despite increasing inputs.
elimination_hints: Punjab's yield plateau has been extensively discussed in agricultural policy literature.
All four listed degradation causes are standard textbook explanations.
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Evaluate the Assertion and Reason below regarding legume crops in crop rotation and select the option that accurately describes the relationship between them.
assertion: Including legume crops like pulses or soybean in rotation with cereals reduces the nitrogen fertiliser requirement of the succeeding cereal crop.
reason: Legumes form symbiotic associations with Rhizobium bacteria that fix atmospheric nitrogen in root nodules, leaving nitrogen-rich residues in soil for the next crop to utilise.
options: A) Both Assertion and Reason are correct, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
B) Both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
C) Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect
D) Assertion is incorrect
explanation: The assertion is correct — legume rotations can save 20–60 kg N/ha for the next cereal, a well-documented agronomic benefit. The reason correctly explains the mechanism: Rhizobium in root nodules fixes atmospheric N₂ into NH₃, which the plant uses. After harvest, crop residues (root nodules, leaves) decompose, releasing N into soil. This is the scientific basis for cereal-legume rotations like rice-chickpea, maize-soybean, and wheat-pigeonpea.
elimination_hints: Biological nitrogen fixation via Rhizobium-legume symbiosis is foundational agronomy.
The rotation benefit is the single biggest reason for including legumes in cropping systems.
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Read the Assertion and Reason about MSP-based procurement for pulses and oilseeds and determine whether both statements are correct and if the reason explains the assertion appropriately.
assertion: Despite MSP being announced for pulses and oilseeds, actual government procurement at MSP for these crops has historically been much lower than for wheat and rice.
reason: The FCI operational infrastructure is primarily geared toward rice and wheat, while pulses and oilseeds are procured by NAFED and state agencies with limited reach and storage capacity.
options: A) Both Assertion and Reason are correct, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
B) Both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
C) Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect
D) Assertion is incorrect
explanation: The assertion is correct — actual MSP procurement of pulses and oilseeds has historically remained below 10% of production, while wheat and rice procurement often exceeds 30%. The reason correctly explains this: FCI was built primarily for rice-wheat operations with vast depot networks and mandis. Pulses and oilseeds are handled by NAFED, state cooperatives, and agencies with smaller operations and limited storage. The PM-AASHA scheme (2018) attempted to address this gap with PSS, PDPS, and PPPS components.
elimination_hints: FCI's rice-wheat dominance is a well-known structural feature of Indian procurement.
NAFED's smaller scale for pulses/oilseeds explains the procurement gap.
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Arrange the following steps in the correct sequence of hybrid rice seed production using the three-line (A, B, R) system followed in commercial seed production programmes.
items: Develop A line (male sterile), B line (maintainer), and R line (restorer) as parental seed stocks
Cross A line × B line in isolated plots to multiply A line seed for commercial crossing
Cross A line × R line in main hybrid seed production plot using synchronised flowering
Harvest, clean, and package hybrid (F1) seed from A line plants for distribution to farmers
options: A) Parental line development → A×B multiplication → A×R hybrid production → F1 seed harvest & packaging
B) A×R hybrid production → Parental line development → A×B multiplication → F1 seed harvest & packaging
C) A×B multiplication → A×R hybrid production → Parental line development → F1 seed harvest & packaging
D) F1 seed harvest → Parental line development → A×B multiplication → A×R hybrid production
explanation: Hybrid rice seed production follows a strict multi-generation sequence: (1) maintain and purify three parental lines (A = CMS male sterile, B = maintainer to multiply A, R = restorer with Rf gene); (2) multiply A line by crossing A×B (since A is sterile, B pollinates and progeny are also male sterile, enabling multiplication); (3) produce commercial hybrid by crossing A×R in main plot — R pollinates A and produces fertile F1 hybrid seed; (4) harvest A line (which bears the hybrid seed), clean, and distribute. Farmers must purchase fresh F1 seed every season.
elimination_hints: Cannot cross A×R before having pure A line seed — A×B multiplication must come first.
Parental lines must exist before any crossing — development comes first logically.
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Arrange the following operations in the correct chronological order for a typical groundnut cultivation cycle in the kharif season in a semi-arid region of India.
items: Field preparation including deep ploughing and fine tilth seedbed for easy peg penetration
Seed treatment with fungicide (thiram/carbendazim) and Rhizobium culture before sowing
Sowing at 30×10 cm spacing with seed rate of 100 kg/ha (bunch type) during June–July
Gypsum application at flowering stage (30–45 DAS) to ensure proper pod filling and calcium supply
options: A) Field preparation → Seed treatment → Sowing → Gypsum at flowering
B) Seed treatment → Sowing → Field preparation → Gypsum at flowering
C) Sowing → Field preparation → Seed treatment → Gypsum at flowering
D) Gypsum at flowering → Sowing → Seed treatment → Field preparation
explanation: Groundnut cultivation sequence: (1) field preparation is always first — deep ploughing and fine tilth are essential for easy peg penetration during pod formation; (2) seed treatment with fungicide controls collar rot and Rhizobium inoculation enhances N fixation — done before sowing; (3) sowing at proper spacing with appropriate seed rate follows; (4) gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) is applied at flowering/pegging stage to supply calcium essential for pod development — a unique requirement of groundnut.
elimination_hints: Field preparation always precedes any field operation — logical first step.
Gypsum at flowering is a mid-season input — cannot be first or before sowing.
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Evaluate whether the following statement is true or false: India is the world's largest producer of pulses, the world's largest consumer of pulses, and also imports significant quantities of pulses to meet domestic demand for protein requirements.
options: A) True
B) False
explanation: This statement is true. India produces around 25 million tonnes of pulses annually — the world's largest producer (about 25% of global production). Simultaneously, India is the largest consumer due to its vast vegetarian population requiring protein from pulses. Despite domestic production, India imports 2–4 million tonnes of pulses annually (especially chickpea, lentil, and pigeonpea) from Canada, Myanmar, Australia, and African countries to bridge the demand-supply gap. This paradox drives ongoing pulse self-sufficiency initiatives.
elimination_hints: India's dual status as largest producer and largest consumer is a well-known fact.
The import dependency despite production leadership is widely cited in agricultural policy.
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Study the diagram showing major pulses and oilseeds of India along with their leading producing states and seasons, then identify the correct crop-state-season combination as per current agricultural statistics.
options: A) Chickpea — Madhya Pradesh — rabi season with Malwa plateau as core cultivation area
B) Soybean — Gujarat — rabi season with Saurashtra as main production zone
C) Groundnut — Uttar Pradesh — kharif season dominated by Indo-Gangetic plains
D) Mustard — Tamil Nadu — kharif season grown mainly in coastal regions
explanation: From the diagram, chickpea is correctly identified with Madhya Pradesh as the leading state, rabi as its season, and Malwa plateau as the core cultivation area. Soybean's top state is MP (not Gujarat) and its season is kharif (not rabi). Groundnut's leading state is Gujarat (not UP) with Saurashtra being its main zone. Mustard's leading state is Rajasthan (not Tamil Nadu) and it is a rabi crop (not kharif). Only option A matches all three attributes correctly.
elimination_hints: Saurashtra is associated with groundnut in Gujarat — not soybean.
Mustard is quintessentially a rabi crop — never kharif.
subject: Agriculture
